7 min read
10 hours ago

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Open your app. Go to any search box. Open the network tab.
Now type the word “angular” and count the requests.
If you counted seven, this article is about your code.
One request per keystroke. Seven requests for one word. Six of them wasted, and one of them, the most dangerous one, possibly showing your user the wrong results. Not slow results. Wrong ones.
I found this exact bug in a production insurance portal serving thousands of agents. The search endpoint was the most expensive query in the system, and we were hammering it seven times for every word typed. The backend team had spent two sprints trying to optimize the query. The actual fix was two lines of frontend code.
This is the full anatomy of that mistake: why it happens, the race condition hiding inside it that almost nobody notices, and how Angular can cancel an HTTP request mid-flight, which most developers do not even know is possible.
Get your network tab ready. We are going to watch requests die, and it is going to feel great.
The Mistake, in Its Natural Habitat
Here is the code I found. You have written some version of this. I certainly have:
